**Lettuce** is a variety of leafy green vegetable in the genus *Lactuca*.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)*[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)*

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

All varieties of lettuce are succulent and crispy, with a high water content,[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2) and most have green leaves.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) The aroma is grassy, and the flavor is mildly bitter,[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2) with anthocyanin-containing red-leafed lettuce having an especially pronounced bitterness.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4) When broken, the central leaf rib produces a milky liquid.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2) Leaves closer to the center of the head are generally paler than the outer ones. When immature, lettuces may be called baby lettuce, and they have smaller leaves and a more tender flavor and texture.[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)

### Varieties

[edit | edit source]

There are somewhere on the order of thousands of lettuce varieties,[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) but many can be sorted into one of four broad categories: romaine, leaf, butterhead, or crisphead.[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) **Romaine** (also called **cos**) grows in elongated, loose heads with narrow leaves and sturdy central ribs.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) It tends to be relatively flavorful.[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)**[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) Leaf** lettuces do not form compact heads,[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) with the leaves instead spreading out—this makes it convenient to pluck a few leaves from the growing plant as desired.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) Some varieties may have red coloration;[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) generally mild flavor that increases in bitterness with age.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) **Butterhead** lettuces form loose, rounded heads,[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) and the leaves form little cups when separated off.[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3) Their leaves are light green, the texture is soft,[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) and the flavor is mild.[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) As the name implies, **crisphead** lettuces have very crisp leaves[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)—iceberg lettuce, which grows in very compacted heads, is a classic example of this variety.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) A visually distinct variety of lettuce is called **stem lettuce**, **celtuce**, or **Chinese lettuce**. This variety is cultivated for its thick fleshy stalk rather than its leaves.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)

* ![Butterhead lettuce](/wiki/File:Lettuce_(4988502260).jpg "Butterhead lettuce")

  Indian lettuce
* ![Stem lettuce](/wiki/File:Raw_lettuce.jpg "Stem lettuce")

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

When selecting lettuces of any variety, look for crisp leaves that have no sign of wilting, drying, yellowing, bruising, rot, or insect damage.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:6-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) Head lettuce should be dense and seem heavy.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) Boxed greens should be examined to check for excess moisture or poor quality leaves at the bottom.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)

Lettuce should be stored, unwashed and uncut,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) in the refrigerator.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) They should be wrapped in plastic or damp towels to prevent wilting,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) but excess moisture will speed rot.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) Head lettuce generally keeps better than leaf lettuce, which is more vulnerable to damage.[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4) The cooler the temperature, the longer the shelf life,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) but freezing will damage lettuce leaves[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) and is not recommended.

## Preparation

[edit | edit source]

To prepare lettuce in most cases, start by separating the individual leaves from each other.**[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)** Iceberg lettuce can be firmly smacked, stem-side down, on a hard surface to loosen and dislodge the core.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Thicker central leaf ribs may be trimmed or cut out if desired.**[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)**[[6]](#cite_note-:7-6) Lettuce must then be washed before serving to remove any traces of dirt and grit,[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) then dried well to help dressing stick (if applicable).[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1) Salad spinners are ideal tools to remove excess water.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

![](/wiki/File:Slow_Cooker_Asian_Chicken_Lettuce_Wraps_(36167537862).jpg)

Lettuce wraps

Lettuce is a very popular green and is eaten both raw and cooked. Used raw, lettuce primarily contributes textural contrast through its crisp refreshing characteristics.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) For example, it may be included within sandwiches, wraps, and rolls;[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) it may also be used to wrap or contain other foods,[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) as in Korean *ssam*. Lettuce is very popular in green salads,[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7) such as Caesar salad and Waldorf salad.[[2]](#cite_note-:3-2) In many cuisines, lettuce is also cooked, whereupon it carries flavor and provides a tender and mellow vegetal crunch.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:9-11) Depending on the variety and preparation, it can take well to different cooking styles, including grilling, braising, stir-frying, blanching, and inclusion in soups.[[7]](#cite_note-:4-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10)[[11]](#cite_note-:9-11) Generally speaking, sturdier varieties of lettuce such as romaine or a-choy are best suited for cooking, as they will withstand the heat better.[[10]](#cite_note-:8-10)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Depending on the variety of lettuce and the dish, other leafy greens may be substituted for the lettuce. You'll want to match the general texture and flavor profile.
